comments:
Summary:
The manuscript proposes a simple, principled kernel-design method for SVMs that incorporates knowledge of a sensing model p(x|z) linking observations x to latent states z. The core observation is that the Bayes-optimal decision is linear in the function p(x|z) (viewed as a vector in L2(Z)), and the representer/maximum-margin argument leads to the kernel K(x,x') = ∫ p(x|z)p(x'|z) dz. The authors derive a closed-form sensing-aware kernel for the Bag-of-Words (multinomial) model (expressed via Gamma/factorial terms), propose practical approximations to avoid numerical issues, and evaluate on text (20 Newsgroups) and image (scene) classification tasks, comparing to several baselines including RBF, PPK, diffusion kernels, discriminative topic models, and some deep-learning and vision-specific methods.

Strengths:
- The idea is conceptually clean and connects Bayes optimality with kernel methods in a straightforward way; the derivation is easy to follow and broadly applicable whenever p(x|z) is available.
- For BoW the authors provide a closed-form expression and practical approximations; applying the kernel to both text and vision tasks demonstrates breadth.
- Empirical comparisons include sensible baselines and some stronger recent methods; reported gains over standard kernels are encouraging.
- The approach is lightweight compared to complex generative or deep models and can be practically useful when approximate sensing models exist.

Weaknesses and points for improvement:
- The optimization in (3) includes an L1 constraint on w(z) that is subsequently ignored to derive the finite-dimensional SVM formulation. The manuscript should more carefully justify this omission or discuss its implications (e.g., when the constraint is active, effect on optimality).
- The proposed approximations to the factorial/Gamma form (log mapping, resampling, scaling by n) are heuristic. More analysis of their biasing effects, stability, and how to choose n/N would strengthen the contribution. Complexity and numerical stability analysis for large vocabularies or large Ni should be expanded.
- Experiments are described at a high level but the provided excerpt lacks detailed tables, statistical significance tests, run-time/compute cost comparisons, and ablation studies (sensitivity to parameters such as n, N, vocabulary size W, resampling strategy). These are important to assess practical utility.
- The relation to existing model-based kernels (PPK, diffusion, KL-based kernels) is discussed, but a more precise comparison (mathematically and empirically) would clarify when the sensing-aware kernel is expected to be superior.
- The method presumes knowledge of p(x|z). For many real-world problems p(x|z) must be estimated; a discussion of robustness to model misspecification or estimated sensing models is missing.

Minor/comments on presentation:
- The exposition is generally clear; however, some formatting issues appear in the provided text (likely artefacts of the PDF extraction). The reasoning chain from Bayes rule to the kernel is concise and readable.
- It would help readers if code or pseudo-code for computing the BoW kernel and approximations were provided and if computational complexity per kernel evaluation were stated.

Recommendation:
I find this a solid, practically-relevant contribution connecting generative sensing structure and discriminative kernel design. With clearer justification of the L1 omission, more analysis of the approximations, and expanded experimental details (runtime, sensitivity, statistical tests), the work would be stronger. As presented in the supplied text the paper merits acceptance and oral presentation.
